results.possibleIdentity: Arlene Haller was a sculptor active in the New Jersey art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. She is documented as having participated in significant regional exhibitions, including a 1975 juried show at the Summit Art Center (now the Visual Arts Center of New Jersey), where she exhibited in the sculpture category alongside artists such as Rela Banks and Edward M. Goldman. Haller was also a featured artist in the 1977/1978 season (Year 7) of the Mary H. Dana Women Artists Series at Douglass College, Rutgers University, as part of a group exhibition titled "New Jersey Women Artists." This series, which began in 1971, was a pioneer in providing a venue for women artists and was influenced by the broader women's movement of the 1960s. Her work within this artistic community and her activity in the New York-New Jersey area aligns with a potential association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that operated in the same region and period to foster collaborations between artists and the scientific community.

results.possibleIdentity: Sacha Kolin (1911–1981) was a French-born American painter and sculptor who was a notable figure in the mid-to-late 20th-century New York art scene. Born in Paris to Ukrainian Jewish parents and raised in Vienna, she fled Nazi persecution and immigrated to New York City in 1936. Kolin was a multi-disciplinary artist whose style evolved from early abstract compositions and 'Optical Art' (Op Art) paintings to minimalist, geometric, and often monochromatic large-scale sculptures, frequently utilizing the triangle as a primary motif. She was an early and active member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization founded to foster collaborations between artists and engineers; her sculptural work reflected this interest, often featuring machine-tooled techniques and aeronautically inspired designs influenced by her father’s career in aeronautics. Kolin was also a participant in the New York Correspondence School (mail art) and a close friend of Ray Johnson. Despite a prolific career that included 19 solo exhibitions and inclusion in over 100 museum collections, she faced chronic financial hardship and was known for bartering her artwork for rent and essentials. Her monumental aluminum sculpture, 'Drawing in the Sky #1' (1973), remains on permanent display at the Everson Museum of Art in Syracuse, New York.

segment.text: Kolin was a multi-disciplinary artist whose style evolved from early abstract compositions and 'Optical Art' (Op Art) paintings to minimalist, geometric, and often monochromatic large-scale sculptures, frequently utilizing the triangle as a primary motif.

She was an early and active member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization founded to foster collaborations between artists and engineers; her sculptural work reflected this interest, often featuring machine-tooled techniques and aeronautically inspired designs influenced by her father’s career in aeronautics.
Kolin was also a participant in the New York Correspondence School (mail art) and a close friend of Ray Johnson.
